topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the faded photograph on the table—us, grinning wildly at a poorly lit summer fair. My hand trembled, hovering inches above the glossy paper. Liam, my oldest confidant, was sitting across from me, tracing patterns on the condensation of his glass. He laughed, a bright, easy sound that used to anchor me, but now it just felt distant, echoing in an empty room. When I finally spoke, my voice came out thin, reedy. "Remember that trip to the coast? The one where we tried to build that ridiculous sandcastle?" He blinked, a slow, unfocused movement. "A sandcastle? Hmm. I think I was busy." The air seemed to thicken around me, pressing in on my chest. I looked up, searching his eyes for the familiar spark, but found only a vast, empty expanse. My feet felt rooted to the floor, unable to move toward the reality that was slipping away.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the conversation in my head, trying to find the moment the shift occurred. It wasn't a dramatic break; it was a slow erosion, like sand washing away a shoreline. Sarah was telling a story about her new job, her gestures animated, her eyes sparkling with genuine excitement. I offered a small, encouraging nod, waiting for the shared glance, the silent acknowledgment that we were still tethered by years of shared history. But when she paused, waiting for my input, there was nothing. Her gaze swept over me, polite, distant, like acknowledging a stranger who happened to share a hallway. A coldness seeped into my limbs, making my skin prickle. I tried to interject, to pull her back into our orbit, but the words caught in my throat, lodging there like jagged stones. I just watched her, a strange, hollow ache settling deep in my stomach, as if a vital part of me had simply been erased from the landscape.

[story 3]

The hallway smelled faintly of lemon polish and old wood. I clutched the worn backpack straps so tightly my knuckles were white. I found Mark leaning against the lockers, talking animatedly to another group. I took a breath, rehearsing the opening line—something about the old arcade games, something concrete. When I finally managed to approach, my voice sounded unnaturally loud in the quiet corridor. "Hey, Mark. Remember that time we tried to sneak into the abandoned theater?" He turned, his expression shifting from engaged to mildly confused. He frowned slightly, tilting his head as if trying to place a distant memory. "Theater? Sorry, I don't think I know you. Are you new around here?" The sound of his voice, devoid of recognition, struck me like a physical blow. My vision swam momentarily, the bright fluorescent lights above seeming too harsh, too sharp. I took an involuntary step back, my heart hammering against my ribs with a frantic, uneven rhythm. Everything felt tilted, the floor beneath my sneakers seeming unsteady, as if the very ground had forgotten my presence.
